result:
Done. The spec is fully implemented in `CsvRow.calr`, the project builds cleanly (0 warnings, 0 errors), and behavior is verified end-to-end — all in 1 of the 10 build cycles.

The task turned out to be in **Calor**, a custom tag-based language (`.calr`) that compiles to C# through a custom MSBuild SDK, so I first studied its syntax reference and compiler source (notably: `§L` for-loop bounds are inclusive, effect declarations are enforced with declared ⊇ inferred, and `§PUSH` on a list infers the `mut` effect). I then added three functions alongside the existing `NeedsQuoting` (untouched):

- **`EscapeField`** (`§E{}`, pure) — wraps the field in quotes with internal quotes doubled when `NeedsQuoting` holds, otherwise returns it verbatim.
- **`JoinRow`** (`§E{}`, pure) — escapes each field and joins with commas via a first-element flag; the empty list yields the empty string.
- **`SplitRow`** (`§E{mut}`, required by the enforcer because it builds a `List<str>` with `§PUSH`; still pure computation with no I/O or global state) — a while-loop parser: a field starting with `"` is scanned to its un-doubled closing quote, copying contiguous segments with `substr` and turning each doubled quote into one literal quote; unquoted fields run to the next comma or end of line; a trailing comma produces a final empty field, and the empty line parses to one empty field.

To verify actual behavior (not just compilation), I inspected the generated C# under `obj/calor/` and ran a throwaway console harness in /tmp (since the fixture can't gain test files) against the built `Src.dll`: all directed edge cases passed (empty line, trailing comma, doubled quotes, CR/LF content, `JoinRow([]) == ""`), plus 20,000 randomized `SplitRow(JoinRow(fields)) == fields` round-trips over a quote/comma/CRLF-heavy alphabet — all passed. The scratch harness was deleted afterward.
